audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear a man talking to the coordinator of a photography club.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Wayside Camera Club, Breda speaking.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Um. My name is Dan, and I'd like to join your club.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: That's great, Dan.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: We have an application form.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Would you like to complete it over the phone?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Then you can ask any questions you might have.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: OK, so what's your family name?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: It's Green, Dan Green.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: So, can I take your email address?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Yes, it's dan1068@market.com.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Thanks, and what about your home address?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Well, I'm about 10 miles away from your club in Peacetown.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I live in a house there.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: OK, so what's the house number and street?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: It's 52 Marrowfield Street.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Is that MARROWFIELD?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: And that's Peacetown, you said?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: So, how did you hear about our club?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Did you look on the internet?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: I usually do that, but this time well, I was talking to a relative the other day, and he suggested it.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Oh, is he a member too?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: He belongs to another club, but he'd heard good things about yours.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: OK, so what do you hope to get from joining?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Well, one thing that really interests me is the competitions that you have.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I enjoy entering those.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Right, anything else?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Well, I also like to socialize with other photographers.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: That's great.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: So, what type of membership would you like?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: What are the options?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: It's £30 a year for full membership, or £20 a year if you're an associate.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: I think I'll go for the full membership then.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: That's a good idea, because you can't vote in meetings with an associate membership.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: If I could just find out a bit more about you.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: So, you said you wanted to compete.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Have you ever won any photography competitions?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Not yet, but I have entered 3 in the past.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's interesting.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: So why don't you tell me something about those?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Let's start with the first one.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Well, the theme was entitled "Domestic life".
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I see, so it had to be something related to the home.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Yeah, I chose to take a photo of a family sitting around the dinner table having a meal.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: And I didn't win, but I did get some feedback.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Oh, what did the judges say?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: That it was too busy as a picture.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Aha - so it was the composition of the picture that they criticized?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: And once they told me that, I could see my mistake.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: So what was the theme of the second competition?
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Well, my university was on the coast, and that area gets a lot of beautiful sunsets.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: So that was the theme.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Oh sunsets, that's a great theme.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Yes, the instructions were to capture the clouds as well.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Uh. It couldn't just be blue sky and a setting sun.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Sure, cause they give you all those amazing pinks and purples.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Yeah, and I thought I'd done that well, but the feedback was that I should have waited a bit longer to get the shot.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: So the timing wasn't right.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Yes, I took it too soon, basically.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: And then the third competition I entered was called Animal Magic.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Well, that's a difficult subject.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Ha, I know.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I had to take hundreds of shots.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I'm sure, because animals move all the time.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: That's what we had to show.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: There had to be some movement in the scene.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I got a great shot of a fox in the end, but I took it at night, and well, I suspected that it was a bit dark, which is what I was told.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Well, Dan, you seem to be really keen, and we'd be delighted to have you in our club.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: I'm sure we can help with all those areas that you've outlined.
audioscript.speaker: BREDA

audioscript.text: Thanks, that's great.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Photography competitions

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear an expert on picking wild mushrooms called Dan talking on the radio.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: This evening, we're delighted to welcome Dan Beagle, who's just written a book on looking for and finding food in the wild.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: He's going to tell us everything we need to know about picking wild mushrooms.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: Thank you very much.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Well, I need to start by talking about safety.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: You really need to know what you're doing because some mushrooms are extremely poisonous.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Having said that, once you know what to look for it's really worth doing for the amazing variety of mushrooms available, which you can't get in the shops.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: But of course you have to be very careful.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: And that's why I always say you should never consume mushrooms picked by friends or neighbors.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Always remember that some poisonous mushrooms look very similar to edible ones.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: And it's easy for people to get confused.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: The other thing to avoid is mushrooms growing beside busy roads for obvious reasons, but nothing beats the taste of freshly picked mushrooms.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Don't forget that the ones in the shops are often several days old and past their best.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: There are certain ideas about wild mushrooms that it's important to be aware of.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Don't listen to people who tell you that it's only OK to eat mushrooms that are pale.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Or dull.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: This is completely untrue.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Some edible mushrooms are bright red for example.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Personally I prefer mushrooms cooked, but it won't do you any harm to eat them uncooked in salads.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: It's not necessary to peel them.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Another thing you should remember is that you can't tell if a mushroom is safe to eat by its smell.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Some of the most deadly mushrooms have no smell.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: And taste quite nice, apparently.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Finally, just because deer or squirrels eat a particular mushroom, doesn't mean that you can.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Of course, mushroom picking is associated with the countryside, but if you haven't got a car, your local park can be a great place to start.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: There are usually a range of habitats where mushrooms grow, such as playing fields and wooded areas, but you need to be there first thing in the morning.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: As there's likely to be a lot of competition, not just from people, but wildlife too.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: The deer often get the best mushrooms in my local park.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: If you're a complete beginner, I wouldn't recommend going alone or relying on photos in a book, even the one I've written.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: There are some really good phone apps for identifying mushrooms, but you can't always rely on getting a good signal in the middle of a wood.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: If possible, you should go with a group led by an expert.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: You'll stay safe, and learn a lot that way.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Conservation is a really important consideration, and you must follow a few basic rules.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: You should never pick all the mushrooms in one area.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Collect only enough for your own needs.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Be very careful that you don't trample on young mushrooms or other plants.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: And make sure you don't pick any mushrooms that are endangered and protected by law.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: There's been a decline in some varieties of wild mushrooms in this part of the country.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Restaurants are becoming more interested in locally sourced food like wild mushrooms.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: But the biggest problem is that so many new houses have been built in this area in the last 10 years, and more water is being taken from rivers and reservoirs.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Because of this, mushroom habitats have been destroyed.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Anyway, a word of advice on storing mushrooms.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Collect them in a brown paper bag, and as soon as you get home, put them in the fridge.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: They'll be fine for a couple of days, but it's best to cook them as soon as possible.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: After washing them really carefully first, of course.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: So, everybody knows what a mushroom tastes like, right?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Well, you'll be surprised by the huge variety of wild mushrooms there are.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Be adventurous.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: They're great in so many dishes, stir fries, risottos, pasta.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Just be aware that some people can react badly to certain varieties, so it's a good idea not to eat huge quantities to begin with.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: OK, so now I'm going to show you a few examples of poisonous...
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You'll stay safe, and learn a lot that way.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Conservation is a really important consideration, and you must follow a few basic rules.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: You should never pick all the mushrooms in one area.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Collect only enough for your own needs.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Be very careful that you don't trample on young mushrooms or other plants.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: And make sure you don't pick any mushrooms that are endangered and protected by law.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: There's been a decline in some varieties of wild mushrooms in this part of the country.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Restaurants are becoming more interested in locally sourced food like wild mushrooms.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: But the biggest problem is that so many new houses have been built in this area in the last 10 years, and more water is being taken from rivers and reservoirs.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Because of this, mushroom habitats have been destroyed.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Anyway, a word of advice on storing mushrooms.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Collect them in a brown paper bag, and as soon as you get home, put them in the fridge.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: They'll be fine for a couple of days, but it's best to cook them as soon as possible.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: After washing them really carefully first, of course.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: So, everybody knows what a mushroom tastes like, right?
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Well, you'll be surprised by the huge variety of wild mushrooms there are.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Be adventurous.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: They're great in so many dishes, stir fries, risottos, pasta.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: Just be aware that some people can react badly to certain varieties, so it's a good idea not to eat huge quantities to begin with.
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: OK, so now I'm going to show you a few examples of poisonous...
utterances.speaker: DAN

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO warnings does Dan give about picking mushrooms?
options: Don't pick more than one variety of mushroom at a time.
Don't pick mushrooms near busy roads.
Don't eat mushrooms given to you.
Don't eat mushrooms while picking them.
Don't pick old mushrooms.

body.title: Which TWO ideas about wild mushrooms does Dan say are correct?
options: Mushrooms should always be peeled before eating.
Mushrooms eaten by animals may be unsafe.
Cooking destroys toxins in mushrooms.
Brightly coloured mushrooms can be edible.
All poisonous mushrooms have a bad smell.

items.title: What advice does Dan give about picking mushrooms in parks?
options: Choose wooded areas.
Don't disturb wildlife.
Get there early.

items.title: Dan says it is a good idea for beginners to
options: use a mushroom app.
join a group.
take a reference book.

items.title: What does Dan say is important for conservation?
options: selecting only fully grown mushrooms
picking a limited amount of mushrooms
avoiding areas where rare mushroom species grow

items.title: According to Dan, some varieties of wild mushrooms are in decline because there is
options: a huge demand for them from restaurants.
a lack of rain in this part of the country.
a rise in building developments locally.

items.title: Dan says that when storing mushrooms, people should
options: keep them in the fridge for no more than two days.
keep them in a brown bag in a dark room.
leave them for a period after washing them.

items.title: What does Dan say about trying new varieties of mushrooms?
options: Experiment with different recipes.
Expect some to have a strong taste.
Cook them for a long time.

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two business students discussing automation and the future of work.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: That seminar yesterday on automation and the future of work was really good, wasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Looking at the first industrial revolution in Britain in the 19th century.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: And seeing how people reacted to massive change was a real eye-opener.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it was interesting to hear how people felt about automation then, and what challenges they faced.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: I didn't know that first started with workers in the textile industry.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: With those protesting workers called the Luddites, destroying their knitting machines because they were so worried about losing their jobs.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: And ultimately, they didn't achieve anything.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: And anyway, industrialization created more jobs than it destroyed.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's true.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: But it probably didn't seem a positive thing at the time.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: I can see why the Luddites felt so threatened.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: I know, I'm sure I would have felt the same.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: The discussion about the future of work was really optimistic for a change.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: I like the idea that work won't involve doing boring repetitive tasks, as robots will do all that.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: Normally, you only hear negative stuff about the future.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: Bit too optimistic, don't you think?
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: For example, I can't see how people are about to have more leisure time, when all the evidence shows people are spending longer than ever at work.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: No, that's true.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: And what about lower unemployment?
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm not so sure about that.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: Perhaps in the long term, but not in the foreseeable future.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Mmm.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: And I expect most people will be expected to work until they're much older.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: As everyone's living much longer.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's already happening.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I enjoyed all that stuff on how technology has changed some jobs, and how they're likely to change in the near future.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, incredible, like accountants.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: You might think all the technological innovations would have put them out of a job, but in fact there are more of them than ever.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: They're still really in demand and have become far more efficient.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: That was amazing.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: 20 times more accountants in this country compared to the 19th century.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: I know.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: I'd never have thought the demand for hairdressing would have gone up so much in the last hundred years.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: One hairdresser for every 287 people now, compared to one for over 1,500.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, because people's earning power has gone up, so they can afford to spend more on personal services like that.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: But technology hasn't changed the actual job that much.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: No, they've got hair dryers et cetera, but it's one job where you don't depend on a computer.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: The kind of work that administrative staff do has changed enormously,
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: thanks to technology. Even 20 years ago, there were secretaries doing dictation and typing.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, really boring compared to these days, when they're given much more responsibility and higher status.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Mmm.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: A lot of graduates go in for this kind of work now...
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'd expected there to be a much bigger change in the number of agricultural workers in the 19th century, but the 1871 census showed that roughly 25% of the population worked on the land.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, I'd have assumed it would be more than 50%.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Now it's less than 0.2% (nought point two percent).
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, what about care workers?
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: They barely existed in the 19th century, as people's lifespan was so much shorter, but now of course this sector will see huge growth.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, and it's hard enough to meet current demand.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: The future looks quite bleak for bank clerks.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: They've been in decline since ATMs were introduced in the 80s, and technology will certainly make most of the jobs they do now redundant, I think.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG MAN

audioscript.text: I agree.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: Although the situation may change, it's very hard to predict what will happen.
audioscript.speaker: YOUNG WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO opinions about the Luddites do the students express?
options: Their actions were ineffective.
They are still influential today.
They have received unfair criticism.
They were proved right.
Their attitude is understandable.

body.title: Which TWO predictions about the future of work are the students doubtful about?
options: Work will be more rewarding.
Unemployment will fall.
People will want to delay retiring.
Working hours will be shorter.
People will change jobs more frequently.

text: What comment do the students make about each of the following jobs?
textReadable: What comment do the students make about each of the following jobs?

list: These jobs are likely to be at risk.
Their role has become more interesting in recent years.
The number of people working in this sector has fallen dramatically.
This job will require more qualifications.
Higher disposable income has led to a huge increase in jobs.
There is likely to be a significant rise in demand for this service.
Both employment and productivity have risen.
body.title: Jobs

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear part of a lecture for astronomy students about the need for a system to manage satellites and other objects orbiting the Earth.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In today's astronomy lecture, I'm going to talk about the need for a system to manage the movement of satellites and other objects in orbit around the Earth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In other words, a Space Traffic Management system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We already have effective Air Traffic Control systems that are used internationally to ensure that planes navigate our skies safely.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, Space Traffic Management is a similar concept, but focusing on the control of satellites.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The aim of such a system would be to prevent the danger of collisions in space, between the objects in orbit around the Earth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In order to do this, we'd need to have a set of legal measures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And we'd also have to develop the technical systems to enable us to prevent such accidents, but unfortunately, at present, we don't actually have a Space Traffic Management system that works.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So why not?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What are the problems in developing such a system?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, for one thing, satellites are relatively cheap these days.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Compared with how they were in the past, meaning that more people can afford to put them into space.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So there's a lot more of them out there, and people aren't just launching single satellites.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But whole constellations consisting of thousands of them designed to work together, so space is getting more crowded every day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But in spite of this, one thing you may be surprised to learn is that you can launch a satellite into space, and once it's out there, it doesn't have to send back any information to Earth to allow its identification.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So while we have international systems for ensuring we know where the planes in our skies are, and to prevent them from colliding with one another.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When it comes to the safety of satellites, at present we don't have anything like enough proper ways of tracking them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it isn't just entire satellites that we need to consider.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A greater threat is the huge amount of space debris in orbit around the Earth, broken bits of satellite and junk from space stations and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And some of these are so small that they can be very hard to identify, but they can still be very dangerous.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In addition, some operators may be unwilling to share information about the satellites they've launched.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, a satellite may be designed for military purposes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or it may have been launched for commercial reasons, and the operators don't want competitors to have information about it, and even if the operators are willing to provide it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The information isn't easy to collect.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Details are needed about the object itself, as well as about its location at a particular time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And remember that a satellite isn't very big, and it's likely to be moving at thousands of kilometers an hour.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We don't have any sensors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That can constantly follow something moving so fast, so all that the scientists can do is to put forward a prediction concerning where the satellite is heading next.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So those are some of the problems that we're facing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's consider now some of the solutions that have been suggested.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One key issue is the way in which information is dealt with.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We need more information, but it also needs to be accessible at a global level.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So we need to establish shared standards that we can all agree on for the way in which this information is presented.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We already do this in other areas of science.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So although this is a challenge, it's not an impossible task.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then as all this information's collected, it needs to be put together so it can be used.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And that will involve creating a single database on which it can be entered, as we continue to push forward new developments, congestion of the space environment is only going to increase.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To cope with this, we need to develop a system like the one I've described to coordinate the work of the numerous spacecraft operators.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it's also essential that this system is one that establishes trust in the people that use it, both nationally and at a global level.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One interesting development that I'll mention...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And some of these are so small that they can be very hard to identify, but they can still be very dangerous.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In addition, some operators may be unwilling to share information about the satellites they've launched.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, a satellite may be designed for military purposes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or it may have been launched for commercial reasons, and the operators don't want competitors to have information about it, and even if the operators are willing to provide it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The information isn't easy to collect.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Details are needed about the object itself, as well as about its location at a particular time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And remember that a satellite isn't very big, and it's likely to be moving at thousands of kilometers an hour.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We don't have any sensors.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That can constantly follow something moving so fast, so all that the scientists can do is to put forward a prediction concerning where the satellite is heading next.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So those are some of the problems that we're facing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's consider now some of the solutions that have been suggested.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One key issue is the way in which information is dealt with.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We need more information, but it also needs to be accessible at a global level.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So we need to establish shared standards that we can all agree on for the way in which this information is presented.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We already do this in other areas of science.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So although this is a challenge, it's not an impossible task.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then as all this information's collected, it needs to be put together so it can be used.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And that will involve creating a single database on which it can be entered, as we continue to push forward new developments, congestion of the space environment is only going to increase.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To cope with this, we need to develop a system like the one I've described to coordinate the work of the numerous spacecraft operators.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But it's also essential that this system is one that establishes trust in the people that use it, both nationally and at a global level.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One interesting development that I'll mention...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: is a concept similar to Air Traffic Control, but for satellites rather than planes.
would aim to set up legal and <input> ways of improving safety.
does not actually exist at present.

items: Satellites are now quite <input> and therefore more widespread (e.g. there are constellations made up of <input> of satellites).
At present, satellites are not required to transmit information to help with their <input>.
There are few systems for <input> satellites.
Small pieces of debris may be difficult to identify.
Operators may be unwilling to share details of satellites used for <input> or commercial reasons.
It may be hard to collect details of the object's <input> at a given time.
Scientists can only make a <input> about where the satellite will go.
items.title: Problems in developing effective Space Traffic Management

items: Common standards should be agreed on for the presentation of information.
The information should be combined in one <input>.
A coordinated system must be designed to create <input> in its users.
